parts.text:
The letter is folded on your desk.

Cal is still on his side of the room.

You get up.

This is not you looking back. This is where you are right now. Don't describe where you are. Think from where you are.

Write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] only when you have nothing more.

**Processing the Fallout**

Okay, so the letter's here. *Insufficient evidence*. Just a printed-out email, or maybe a crisp, official-looking university envelope. It doesn't matter, it's just words. They put it in a sentence that's meant to soften the blow, but it doesn't. *The panel notes that an inability to reach a finding does not constitute a determination that the complainant's account is not credible.* Like that makes it better. It means they're not going to do anything. It means I'm back where I started. It means *nothing* happened.

I needed someone else to acknowledge it, to take it from me and say, "Yes, this is real, and we'll fix it." Now it's mine again. Just mine.

Cal's over there. I can feel him, even without looking. He's on his bed, pretending to read. He hasn't turned a page in what feels like an hour. He's careful. I know he’s watching, even if he doesn't want me to know it. He's become a master of peripheral vision. He can read my body language now. He knows I've frozen. He's making himself as small as possible so he doesn't take up any space.

I want him to come over. I want him to get up, walk over, sit beside me, and just *be* there. I want him to be angry, even. That's what he *should* do, what friends do. But if he moves, my chest will tighten. My breathing will shallow. My body... it will react. It will read that movement as a threat. He knows. I know. I hate that I know.

He's not stupid. He'll stop halfway across the room and apologize. He'll apologize for *existing* around me. I can't take that today. I really can't.

So I fold the letter. Once. Twice. Just a piece of paper, folded up. I put it back on the desk.

He turns a page in his book. He doesn't read that one either.

We just sit here.

*Insufficient evidence.*

It’s typed in standard font. Twelve point. Black ink. 

*The panel notes that an inability to reach a finding does not constitute a determination that the complainant's account is not credible.*

They put it in a sentence that cancels itself out. A neat little legal circle. It means: we are not saying you are lying, but we are not doing anything about it. 

It means I keep it. 

I just wanted it out of my head. That was the whole point. I needed someone outside of this room to take it, look at it, and say, *Yes. We see it. This is a thing that happened, and it was wrong.* But they just folded it back up and slipped it under my door. It’s mine again. It's only mine.

Cal is breathing very quietly. 

He’s on his bed. He’s pretending to read, but he’s been on the same page of his textbook since I opened the envelope. He is a master of peripheral vision now. He knows exactly how I’m sitting. He knows I’ve stopped moving. 

I want him to come over here. I want my best friend. I want him to cross the four feet of carpet between us, pull up a chair, and be angry for me. I want to pass him the paper and let him hold it so I don't have to anymore.

But if he moves. If he closes his book, stands up, and takes a step toward me. 

My pulse will spike. My lungs will forget how to pull air. My skin will freeze. My brain will say *it's Cal, it's Cal, it's Cal*, and my body will not listen. My body will just see a man getting closer, and it will shut all the doors. 

And Cal will see it happen. He will see my shoulders lock. He will stop halfway across the rug, his hands twitching at his sides, and he will step back. He will retreat into his corner, folding himself up, making himself as small and unthreatening as possible. He will apologize just by the way he sits.

I can't watch him do that right now. I am so tired of making him do that. 

So I don't say anything. I fold the paper in half. Once. Twice. I set it next to my laptop. 

Across the room, Cal carefully turns a page. He doesn't read that one, either.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**The Weight of the Room**

This goddamn letter...it’s sitting there, folded on the desk, a physical manifestation of a reality I can't quite grasp. Cal’s pretending to read, but I know he’s not. He's hyper-aware of me, of everything. The silence is crushing. I *need* to move, to break the tension without setting him off. God, it's exhausting, this careful dance. I can't be some kind of explosion.

I hate this. I *need* routine. *He* needs routine. Coffee. The walk. I push my chair back. The scrape of the legs on the floor seems amplified, each sound measured. Cal’s posture shifts, a subtle tightening I see from the corner of my eye. I stand. If I stay here, that paper will shrink the room until it crushes us both. 

"I'm going to get coffee," I say, and my voice sounds flat, like I'm talking about the weather. I don't ask if he's coming. That makes it a thing. A decision. I just state a fact. He closes his book, that careful, controlled movement. He doesn't snap it shut. "I'll come." he says. He’s already given me the right-of-way, waiting for me to establish the path so he doesn't accidentally get too close. The math of it is so exhausting.

I put on my coat, don't look at the paper. If I look, it becomes real again, more than just a shape. I grab my keys and walk out. The door. I hear him behind me, the exact distance, a precise, measured, safe three feet. Always. Cold air hits my face as I step out. I breathe it in. The cold is good. Undeniable. A physical fact that’s not this mess.

We walk out into the gray, flat sky. The walk is a given. The crack in the sidewalk. I step over it, he steps over it. There is a hole between us now that would easily fit a third person. We fall into a nothing conversation. It's perfect. The café is four blocks away. I am going to order coffee with oat milk. My hands are going to burn. The routine can carry us for a while. It has to.

I push my chair back. 

It scrapes against the linoleum. Loud. Too loud. 

Cal's posture stiffens, just for a second, before he forces his shoulders to drop. He keeps his eyes on his book. I know he’s not looking at the words. He's mapping my trajectory. He's waiting to see where I go so he can make sure he's not in the way.

I stand up. 

The room feels thick. Like breathing underwater. If I stay in here with that piece of paper, I am going to suffocate, and I'm going to take him down with me.

I need a routine. I need a fact.

"I'm going to get coffee," I say. 

My voice is level. It sounds like a Tuesday. It sounds like before. 

Cal closes the textbook. Carefully. He doesn't snap it shut. "I'll come."

He stands up, but he doesn't walk toward the door. He waits by his bed. He is giving me the right of way. He is letting me establish the route so he doesn't accidentally cross too close to my orbit. 

I hate it. I hate the math of it. Three feet. Four feet. Calculate the angle of approach. 

I grab my coat. I don't look at the letter. If I look at the letter, it becomes real again. Right now, it's just a shape on a desk. 

I walk out into the hallway. 

I hear Cal's footsteps behind me. One, two, three beats later. The exact right distance. Close enough to be with me. Far enough away that my lungs don't freeze.

The air in the stairwell is colder. I breathe it in. 

We walk out the double doors of the dorm. The sky is gray and flat, exactly like home. We take the path past the science building. We step over the place where the roots of the oak tree have pushed the concrete up. I step over it. A second later, he steps over it.

He walks beside me now, but with a gap between us that could fit a whole third person. 

"It's cold," he says.

"Yeah."

It’s a nothing conversation. It’s perfect. It’s exactly what works. 

My body is unclenching out here. The air is sharp. The coffee shop is exactly four blocks away. I am going to order coffee with a splash of oat milk. I am going to hold the paper cup until it burns my palms, because that is a sensation I can control. 

I am going to let the routine do the heavy lifting. I am going to put one foot in front of the other, and Cal is going to walk exactly far enough away, and we are going to pretend for twenty minutes that there is nothing folded up on my desk waiting for me to come back.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Analyzing the Scene: A Coffee Shop Interlude**

Okay, so the instructions are clear: Continue the scene or say it's done. I'm not done. Nora and Cal are at the coffee shop. My goal is to keep things moving naturally, and stick with Nora’s perspective, her immediate reality, staying in the present.

First, the arrival: What are the sensory details of the place? The bell, the smell, the line, the ordering – these are grounding. They’re a way to create contrast between the safety of the routine and that underlying tension. Cal's carefulness needs to be maintained. I need to make the point that the routine is safe, but the letter is still in her room.

So, I’m building this scene in stages:

Arrival: The bell, always the bell. The smell – roasted beans, stale pastries. It's safe.

Ordering: It's a script. "Small dark roast, splash of oat milk." The barista is unaware of anything beyond this transaction. I’m Nora. The relief of the barista's ignorance is welcome. I’ll order my own, and have Cal get his. Independent, but together.

Waiting: It’s a tight space. This is where the distance math becomes critical. The physical reaction is important. Maybe someone brushes past, or Cal has to move. What happens? Does he get too close? I'll let the crowded shop play this out. Students, loud, taking up space. Cal has to move closer to make room, but does he put himself in the gap? The body vs. mind reaction is what's important here.

Refining my internal monologue: Short, factual sentences. The focus needs to be on Cal. She feels guilty. I want the reader to feel it.

Writing: The bell chimes. It always chimes. That’s good. The line. I’ll make the line a specific thing. Gives me something to look at. Cal is the buffer. The order is a rote thing. It should be comforting. The barista doesn't know. The barista doesn't know the words. She is a small dark roast to the barista. I love her for it. The space, the mat. He moves to stand near me, stops at the mat. Too close. Containment. The stumble. Inside her space. Automatic. Horror on Cal's face. The panic. The burn. The routine failed.

Now, a quick self-check to ensure this captures the right persona. Quiet, observant, factual. Hates what her body does to him. Sensory detail. Perfect spatial dynamics.

And now, I'll write the scene.

The bell above the door chimes. It always chimes. That’s good.

The line is three people deep. Also good. It gives me something to look at. The menu board I already have memorized. The pastry case with the scones that are always a little too dry. 

Cal stands at the edge of the line. He’s pretending to look at the bags of coffee beans on the retail shelf, but he’s keeping himself positioned between me and the door. A buffer. 

I step up to the register. 

"Small dark roast. Splash of oat milk." 

The barista taps the screen. She doesn't look up. She has a smudge of blue pen on her thumb. She doesn't know about the letter. She doesn't know what the words *insufficient evidence* look like. To her, I am just a small dark roast. I love her for it.

I pay and step to the side. 

Cal orders a green tea. He pays for it, then moves to the pickup counter. The space here is tighter. The floor plan is narrow. He stops at the edge of the rubber mat. Two feet away. It's closer than we were outside, but there are people behind us. He puts his hands in his coat pockets. He is making himself contained. He is pulling his elbows in. 

A group of guys walks in. They are loud. They are taking off backpacks, shifting around, not looking where they're stepping. 

One of them backs up and bumps hard into Cal. 

Cal stumbles forward. A half-step. He catches his balance, but it puts him right inside my space. His sleeve brushes my arm. 

My breath stops. 

It’s automatic. A steel door slamming shut in my chest. All the air leaves the room. My shoulders hit my ears, my spine locks, and I shrink back against the counter, my hands pressing flat and hard into the wood. My eyes wide. 

Cal freezes. He looks horrified. 

"Sorry," he whispers. 

He doesn't mean for the bump. He means for being too close. He means for setting it off. He scrambles backward, pressing his shoulders against the brick wall, putting as much air between us as the crowded shop allows. He looks like I just hit him. 

"It's fine," I say. 

My voice is tight. It sounds small. It doesn't sound like a Tuesday anymore. 

"Small dark roast!" the barista calls out. 

I grab the paper cup. It’s too hot. The sleeve isn't on right, and the cardboard burns the palm of my hand. I hold it tight anyway. The burn is loud. It gives my brain a single, sharp thing to focus on that isn't the sudden lack of oxygen in my lungs. 

"Let's go," I say. 

I walk out. I don't wait to see if he grabbed his tea. I know he’s following. He always follows. 

The cold air hits my face. I take a breath. It shakes. 

I hear his footsteps behind me. They are farther back now. Ten feet. Twelve feet. The gap will be wider all the way back to the dorm. I ruined the walk. 

The routine didn't fix it. It just moved the problem outside.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Internal Analysis: The Aftermath**

Okay, here's where I am. I’ve analyzed the situation: Nora, traumatized, quiet, and careful, and her roommate Cal, her best friend, who is also traumatized by proxy. The directive is clear: stay in this moment, in Nora’s head. This letter… it's the anchor now.

They are walking back from the coffee. The "insufficient evidence" letter is a physical presence now. I need to figure out the internal state. It’s about the burn of the coffee cup, the cold air, the frustrating involuntary physical reaction to Cal, the guilt and how they can't speak about it. The *hope* of external validation is gone. Now it’s just the aftermath and the consequences.

We're back in the dorm. It's the desk, the folded paper. Cal retreats, but I know he's watching. The tension is palpable. My body is a problem. The physical sensation, the details, are all here, grounding the experience.

This isn’t about describing. This is about feeling. Okay, I need to stay tight, short sentences, and focus on the immediate.

*   The wind. The cup. The cold.
*   The silence, and the guilt and the desire to tell Cal it wasn't him.
*   The room and the letter.

I am drafting the scene, the walk back, and their entry into the room. I will have the letter sitting there. I need to capture the claustrophobia, and then the final internal realization. The letter is the evidence and the physical reality of the room, and Cal and Nora's distance, all are a counterpoint to the letter's lie.

I will write the physical action of putting the letter away. A moment of small control, a way to move forward but not erase it. Maybe I will *not* do anything. Let it sit there. The conclusion of this thought will be the moment I come to realize the evidence is not in the letter, but in the distance.

I am writing it now. The wind kicks up, I focus on the heat of the cup, I do not look back. I will add one final action to it - the finality of their new reality. They will get back to their lives.

I need to make the tone precise, tired, and observant, and reflect the endurance of Nora's routine.
I don't loosen my grip on the cup… No. The wind is biting.
Okay, here we go.

I don't loosen my grip on the cup. Let it burn. It keeps me in my feet. It keeps me on the sidewalk. 

The wind kicks up, biting through my coat. I don't look over my shoulder. I don't need to. I know the cadence of his boots on the concrete. He is matching my pace exactly, just dragging a twelve-foot anchor between us. 

I want to turn around. I want to say, *It wasn't you. You know it wasn't you.*

But I don't. Because saying it means we are talking about it, and if we talk about it, the careful architecture we've built to survive this semester might collapse entirely. So I just walk. 

Through the double doors. Up the stairs. Down the hall. 

I unlock our door. Push it open. 

The room is exactly as we left it. The air is stale, smelling faintly of radiator heat and laundry detergent. 

I walk to my desk. I set the coffee down. I don't take off my coat. 

Cal walks in a moment later. The door clicks shut. He moves strictly along the perimeter, tracking the invisible boundary line, and lowers himself onto his bed. He sets his green tea on his nightstand. He hasn't taken a single sip. 

I look at the paper. Folded in half. Resting next to my laptop. 

*Insufficient evidence.* 

They looked at the dates and the timeline and the lack of a torn shirt or a bruised wrist, and they said they couldn't find the proof. 

I look at Cal across the room. He is pressed against the wall, staring at the floor, afraid to breathe too loudly in case it startles me. I look at my own hand, the palm violently red from holding a scalding cup just to feel something louder than my own panic. 

How is this not evidence? 

What do they call this, if not a finding? 

My coffee stops steaming. The radiator hisses. Cal turns a page of a book he isn't reading. 

I open my top drawer. I slide the folded paper inside, underneath a stack of blank notebooks, and I push the drawer shut. It’s a wooden sound. Final. 

I pull my chair out. I sit down.

Some time has passed.

Cal is on his bed. He's doing the thing. The careful thing. The thing where he takes up as little space as possible so you don't have to.

You've been watching him do it for too long.

You get up. You cross the room. You sit on his lap and you hold on.

Your body is doing what it does. You hold on anyway.

This is not you looking back. This is where you are right now. Don't describe where you are. Think from where you are.

Take as many turns as you need, or write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you are done.

**Internal Monologue: Nora's Choice**

Okay, here's the scene. Cal. Small. Careful. Always careful. I'm so done with the careful. It's eating us alive. He sits on his bed, the space of his caution so sharp. I get it. I *see* it. But the space...the silence...it's too much.

So, I'm done. I *move*. He's breathing shallow, knees to his chest. This has to stop. The distance is four steps. He looks up. He expects the usual. Not this. I don't give myself time to change my mind, I don't give my body a chance to react, or even second guess this. I cross the distance and sit on him. It feels odd, out of nowhere.

The alarms. It's like someone flipped a switch in my brain. *Threat. Male. Close. Trapped.* My heartbeat is pounding. My muscles are locking up. Breath is disappearing. It's trying to make me leave. My body is fighting. But I'm staying.

*It's Cal*. Gotta lock this in. He smells like cheap peppermint and books. I dig my fingers in. The grip is key. I'm telling my body that it's safe now. He's rigid, frozen. Scared to touch me, scared to make it worse. He doesn't know what's going on.

*Stay*. I gotta tell myself again. My ribs are creaking, my lungs are shutting down. He starts to let his hands down, but so careful. A hint of contact. A tentative offering. I'm gasping. I'm screaming. It's hard. But I'm taking the hit.

My muscles are begging me to leave. But I'm staying put. This is it. Not going to let the space keep eating us.

"I've got you," he says. His voice is wrecked. I'm still vibrating, but there is this, now, this presence under the static.

I hold on. I don't let go.

His knees are pulled up. He’s taking up maybe two square feet of a mattress that belongs to him. He’s breathing in fractions. 

I am so tired of the math. I am so tired of this invisible tape on the floor.

I stand up. 

He looks up. He expects me to turn toward the door. Or the mini-fridge. He starts to shift his weight, recalculating his buffer zone.

I don't turn. I walk straight at him. 

Three steps. Four. I cross the border.

He freezes. His eyes get wide. He doesn't know what to do with his hands. 

I don't give myself a second to think about the geometry. I just sit down. Right on his lap. I throw my arms around his neck, I press my face into his shoulder, and I hold on.

Instantly, it starts. 

The white noise in my ears. The rushing water. Every alarm bell in my nervous system goes off at once. *Trap. Threat. Danger. Get out. Get out. Get out.* My lungs forget how to work. My spine turns into a steel rod. My skin crawls with ice. My body is frantically trying to eject me from this proximity. It is telling me I am dying.

I dig my fingers into the fabric of his t-shirt. I fist the cotton in both hands. 

I am not moving.

Cal is completely rigid beneath me. A statue. He’s not breathing. His hands are hovering somewhere in the air by my waist, terrified to land, terrified to touch me. He thinks he did something wrong. He thinks this is an accident, or that I'm sleepwalking, or that I'm breaking. 

*It’s Cal,* I tell my brain. *It’s Cal. It’s Cal.*

He smells like the peppermint soap from the dollar store. He smells like the library. He smells safe. 

My heart is battering against my ribs so hard I know he can feel it through our shirts. My whole body is vibrating with the effort of not bolting. I lock my wrists behind his neck. I press my forehead harder into his collarbone. 

*Stay,* I tell my muscles. *You are fine. Stay.*

"Nora," he whispers. His voice is wrecked. Panicked. 

I shake my head against his shoulder. I just squeeze tighter. 

*Hold on. Just hold on.*

Slowly. Centimeter by centimeter. I feel his arms come down. I feel his hands, light as paper, settle on my back. He doesn't pull me in. He doesn't trap me. He just lets me know they are there. A tentative, open-palmed pressure. 

I gasp for air. It’s a ragged, ugly sound. 

My body is screaming. It takes everything I have to keep my knees bent, to keep my arms locked, to stay anchored to him while every biological instinct begs me to run. But I am not letting the room stay divided anymore. I am not letting that piece of paper in the drawer dictate where I am allowed to sit. I am not letting him fold himself into nothing.

I am taking the hit. I am riding it out. 

"I've got you," he whispers into my hair. The words are shaking. "I'm right here. I've got you."

My eyes burn. My chest heaves. The panic is a live wire thrashing in my veins, but underneath it, buried deep beneath the noise and the freeze, is the solid, undeniable weight of my best friend. 

I close my eyes. I hold on.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Internal Analysis & Narrative Construction**

Okay, so the task is to inhabit this moment completely. No looking back, no framing the scene. I *am* Nora, right now, on Cal's lap. The prompt demands present-tense, pure sensory experience mixed with her internal monologue. It's about being *there*, not describing *where* there is. Good. That clarifies the constraints.

My initial assessment of the situation: Nora is enduring a severe trauma response. Panic. Fight or flight, but she’s deliberately fighting it. She loves Cal, deeply, but the trauma is affecting him. Cal is gentle, terrified of hurting her, but wants to offer comfort. My focus needs to be on the physical struggle. The panic needs to be peaking, and then, hopefully, receding just a little. I need to make the contrast between the trauma response and her cognitive truth clear.

The ticking clock, or whatever ambient noise, needs to be juxtaposed with the pounding in her chest. Cal’s presence is an anchor. His hands on her back. She needs to *breathe*. He can help her breathe. The language needs to be choppy, grounded in the body, immediate. Sensory details are key: cotton t-shirt, his heat, her trembling. The comfort of the words "I'm right here."

I have to make the panic break. A wave cresting and rolling back. It won't disappear completely. I know this. Exhaustion needs to be the prevailing state.

Here's how I envision it: the noise *is* the train tunnel. Focus on his hands, the deliberate breath. The fuel is running out of the adrenaline. The exhaustion comes over her. She starts to let go. This is a moment of letting go. The room is still just a room, but the body is finally sitting in the same room as the brain.

This is the draft. Focus on the sharp and rhythmic prose. Make sure the transition from panic to exhaustion is earned and physically grounded. Does it fit Nora's voice? Pragmatic, observant, emotionally exhausted but resolute. Now, let’s inhabit the scene, and see where it goes.

The roar in my ears is so loud it sounds like a train tunnel. 

I focus on his hands. They are planted right in the middle of my back. They aren't pressing. They aren't holding me down. If I shifted my weight, if I pushed back even an inch, his arms would drop. He would let me go instantly. 

That's the only reason I can stay. 

I count the beats of my heart. They are violent. Frantic against my ribs. 

Cal takes a breath. He draws it in slowly, and he lets it out slowly. He does it deliberately. Exaggerated. A metronome. He knows exactly what he’s doing. 

He takes another. His chest rises against mine. I feel the mechanics of it. Bone and muscle. Not a threat. Just Cal. Just the guy who eats dry cereal at two in the morning and leaves his textbooks open on the floor. 

*Breathe in.* 

I try to match his rhythm. I only get a thimbleful of air, catching in my throat. 

*Breathe out.*

He does it again. I trace the smell of peppermint soap. I trace the woven seams of his shirt. 

The live wire in my veins sparks, snaps, and then, very slowly, it begins to hum. 

The panic starts to run out of fuel. Adrenaline can only burn for so long before it eats itself. The ice in my skin melts into a heavy, aching exhaustion. My shoulders drop a fraction of an inch. 

My hands ache. My knuckles are cramped from gripping his shirt so tightly. I force my fingers to loosen, just enough to press my palms flat against his chest instead. 

Cal rests his cheek against the top of my head. He is shaking. I didn't realize that until now. A fine, steady tremor running through his arms and his chest. 

He is so scared for me. He is so relieved. 

"I'm okay," I say. 

It comes out as a rasp. It barely makes it past my teeth. 

"I know," he whispers. He doesn't move. He doesn't change the pressure of his hands. "Take your time."

I let my weight sink. I stop holding my own skeleton rigid. I let gravity work, and I let him hold me. 

The dorm is still just a dorm. The letter is still in the drawer. 

But my body is finally, finally sitting in the same room as my brain.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**My Decision: The Scene's Natural End**

Okay, so the user wants me to either continue or declare I'm done. Let's break this down. Nora's had quite a journey. She’s had a visceral reaction to the letter, that was the initial tension. It was followed by a pretty disastrous attempt at normalcy. Then, the turning point. She forced herself back into the same space as Cal, physically engaging with the trauma response, and finding a safe anchor in the process. The panic was immense, but she got through.

Now? Now, she's letting herself sink into the aftermath. She's found a measure of calm. That last sentence... it’s perfect, isn't it? "My body is finally, finally sitting in the same room as my brain." Captures everything.

To continue... I could, but would it *add* anything? I imagine them pulling apart, talking, falling asleep. But Nora’s strength, her character, has always been about quiet presence, not filling voids. More words, more action, at this point, feels like an over-explanation, a tacked-on epilogue to a perfectly crafted ending. The prompt gives me permission to stop here, and honestly, the emotional arc is complete. This particular interaction has run its course. It’s reached its natural, poignant conclusion. Time to honor that and move on.
